displayTitle: The Thin Layer That Makes It Work

beats.narration: Press a dry probe on steel and the test simply doesn't happen. Trapped air reflects over ninety nine point nine percent of the sound.

payload.title: THE WALL OF AIR
payload.sub: Why sound can't enter the part without a couplant.

beats.narration: A couplant fixes it. A thin water film displaces the air and about twelve percent of the energy crosses — enough to run the test.

payload.heading: The fix: a thin couplant film

beats.narration: Water for smooth surfaces and immersion. Cellulose gel clings for contact work. Glycerin when every decibel counts, near nineteen percent into steel.
On the job, ask four questions: how rough is the surface, which way does it face, how hot, and what alloy?
So the rule: thinnest continuous film, same couplant for calibration and test, clean up after. Cheap gel decides what your instrument sees.

payload.sub: Cheap gel decides what your instrument sees.
